material.mattext:
View stimulus in Figma

There are three links with the same visible text "Learn more." How should the designer annotate these links to conform to WCAG and help screen reader users differentiate them when pulling up a list of links?
Approach 1: Add positional context like "Link one of three"
Approach 2: Associate each link with its adjacent h3 heading text
Approach 3: Associate all links with the page h2 heading
No annotation is needed because the visible text "Learn more" is sufficient
Correct! Approach 2 associates each 'Learn more' link with its adjacent h3 heading, providing the same contextual connection that sighted users get visually.
Incorrect. Positional context like 'Link one of three' doesn't convey meaningful information about the link's purpose.
Incorrect. Associating all links with the same heading doesn't differentiate them at all.
Incorrect. Multiple links with identical text must be differentiated programmatically per SC 2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context).

View stimulus in Figma

A designer has asked you what is the best accessible name to define for the avatar image in this UI. What might you advise them to do here?
Use "[User name] avatar" as the accessible name
Use aria-label="Jack Traven headshot"
Mark the avatar as decorative (no accessible name needed)
Describe the image in detail: "Man looking away from camera in dark background with red lighting"
Correct! The avatar appears adjacent to 'Jack Traven' text, which already identifies the user. Adding an accessible name would create redundancy.
Incorrect. This creates redundancy with the adjacent name text.
Incorrect. This provides redundant information already available from adjacent text.
Incorrect. Detailed visual descriptions are inappropriate for functional UI elements.

View stimulus in Figma

What might you tell a designer about the quality of the accessible name annotation for annotation number three?
It should read "three dots" to describe the visible attributes of the icon
"Menu" is the role of the control and does not belong in the name of the control
It should read "More menu Layer Styles," not Drop Shadow
It should be marked decorative since the entire row is about more options making the control redundant
Correct! 'Menu' is a role, not a name. Screen readers announce roles automatically, so including it creates redundancy.
Incorrect. Accessible names should describe function, not appearance.
Incorrect. 'Drop shadow' correctly identifies which layer style this menu applies to.
Incorrect. The more menu control is not redundant; it provides access to additional options.

View stimulus in Figma

You are evaluating your team member's annotations here. Do you have any notes for them, or is it good to go?
No notes, good to go to engineering as-is
If one graphic is functional, they all are: markers 2, 3, and 5 are all incorrect and need revision
Marker 6 is incidental as opening a new window is non-material to a screen reader user
Marker 5 is incorrect as the warning icon is not decorative
Correct! The warning icon conveys critical status information and must have an accessible name. Relying on color alone violates SC 1.4.1 Use of Color.
Incorrect. There is one issue that needs revision before sending to engineering.
Incorrect. Not all icons must be treated the same way. Each should be evaluated individually.
Incorrect. Opening a new window is material information for screen reader users.

View stimulus in Figma

What might be the best way to specify the accessible name of the button highlighted here?
Copy link [Campaign 1]
Copy link [www.tracking.link/abcdefg12345678]
Copy link
Copy
Correct! 'Copy link [Campaign 1]' describes the function and differentiates it using human-readable context.
Partially correct. The URL differentiates buttons but is not user-friendly.
Incorrect. This doesn't differentiate from the other Copy link button.
Incorrect. 'Copy' is too vague and doesn't differentiate buttons.

View stimulus in Figma

Considering the Design Accessibility Checklist's guidance on headings, would you pass or fail this content?
Pass. All heading text has annotation markers.
Pass. All heading levels/ranks are correctly annotated.
Fail. Some text that is a heading has no annotation marker.
Fail. Some heading levels/ranks are incorrectly annotated.
Correct! The section headings should be H2, not H3. The hierarchy skips from H1 to H3, creating navigation difficulties.
Incorrect. Having markers doesn't mean the levels are correct.
Incorrect. The heading levels are not correctly annotated; they skip from H1 to H3.
Incorrect. All heading text does have annotation markers.

View stimulus in Figma

This is a mobile interface destined for both iOS and Android. What advice would you offer to the designer?
All interfaces must start with an H1 element; we cannot release until you add an H1.
Consider adding a note about Android's support for heading levels/rank.
Android doesn't support heading rank, so do not annotate headings.
The text elements annotated are labels of the groups of controls below, not headings.
Correct! Android TalkBack only supports binary heading identification (on/off), not specific levels. Adding a note ensures proper implementation.
Incorrect. Starting with H1 is best practice but not a strict WCAG requirement.
Incorrect. Android does support headings, just not heading levels.
Incorrect. These text elements function as headings, not labels.

View stimulus in Figma

Run the Image Contrast Scanner on this design. What findings can you report regarding the text elements?
All text contrast passes
The headline text is partially non-conforming
The body text is below contrast minimums
The graphic "Gathering insights" is below contrast minimums
Correct! The body text must meet 4.5:1 contrast but falls below this threshold. The headline passes as large-scale text (3:1).
Incorrect. The body text fails the 4.5:1 requirement for normal-sized text.
Incorrect. The headline passes as large-scale text requiring only 3:1 contrast.
Incorrect. The decorative graphic is not the primary issue here.

View stimulus in Figma

This design has both SC 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) and SC 1.4.1 Use of Color issues. Which element is non-conforming to SC 1.4.1 Use of Color and why?
The Files and Learn buttons; gray means disabled
The Home button; blue means current
The Add button; blue means accent
Trick question, there are no SC 1.4.1 Use of Color issues present
Correct! Blue color alone indicates the Home button is selected, violating SC 1.4.1 Use of Color. A non-color indicator is needed.
Incorrect. Gray for unselected tabs is common and doesn't indicate disabled state.
Incorrect. Blue as an accent color for emphasis is acceptable.
Incorrect. There is a clear SC 1.4.1 Use of Color violation with the Home button.

View stimulus in Figma

Run Stark's color vision simulation tool. Do you observe any color-alone issues in this design?
Yes. The color orange means flashcard
Yes. The color green means quiz
No. The icon colors are over 3:1 to fill color
No. Icons are used in addition to color
Correct! The icons (lightning bolt vs question mark) provide non-color differentiation, satisfying SC 1.4.1 Use of Color.
Incorrect. The lightning bolt icon provides non-color identification.
Incorrect. The quiz indicators use purple, not green, and have a question mark icon.
Incorrect. Contrast ratios don't address SC 1.4.1 Use of Color; the icons provide the differentiation.

item.title: Q11: Non-text Contrast for Component States

material.mattext:
View stimulus in Figma

Would you flag any of these states as non-conforming to SC 1.4.1 Use of Color1 Non-text Contrast?
The selected state only
The selected and hover state only
All three states; the background fills are all under 3:1
None of the states; they're all passing
Correct! The selected state's blue fill at 1.6:1 fails the 3:1 requirement. Hover has no requirement; focus passes.
Incorrect. Hover state doesn't require contrast; the pointer provides visual feedback.
Incorrect. Hover is exempt and focus passes with its blue ring over 3:1.
Incorrect. The selected state fails at 1.6:1 contrast.

View stimulus in Figma

When a user places their cursor in, or gives keyboard focus to, the text field that currently reads "Ask anything," what will happen?
The prompt text will disappear leaving a blinking text insertion cursor
The prompt text will turn into an icon that acts as the label for the text input
The prompt text will move up programmatically and become the label to the text input
The text insertion cursor will start after the prompt text effectively becoming the label
Correct! This is placeholder text as label, which violates SC 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions. The text disappears on focus, leaving no label.
Incorrect. Placeholder text doesn't transform into an icon.
Incorrect. That describes a floating label pattern, which this design doesn't implement.
Incorrect. Placeholder text disappears entirely when the field receives focus.

View stimulus in Figma

Is there sufficient information provided in the UI to understand what the error is and how to fix it?
Yes. The error is the Title formatting doesn't work on Facebook, so turn off Facebook in the publish settings
No. The error doesn't cover the Title formatting for the Adobe products in the publish settings
No. The Toast message doesn't say what's wrong with the Title formatting for the selected platforms in the publish settings
Yes. The error is the Title formatting doesn't work on certain platforms so change the formatting or turn off those platforms in the publish settings
Correct! The design provides sufficient error information to meet SC 3.3.1 Error Identification with clear platform identification and actionable solutions.
Incorrect. The toast identifies YouTube, Twitter, and Vimeo, not Facebook.
Incorrect. The error is about third-party platforms, not Adobe products.
Incorrect. The toast and warning icons provide sufficient information.

View stimulus in Figma

When the user selects "All" from the last Picker, what accessibility challenge will occur?
With no visible text label, they may forget the "All" option is related to Role
Because the text "My Prompts" is above the Picker, they may think the selection is "All My prompts"
The Picker will attempt to display every option in the visible value and cause truncation
Nothing, the Picker will correctly read "Role - All" in the Picker value to preserve context and meaning
Correct! These filters violate SC 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions. When 'All' is selected, the context is lost. Users must remember what each dropdown was for.
Partially correct, but the primary issue is the lack of persistent labels.
Incorrect. 'All' would simply display as 'All' without showing all options.
Incorrect. There is no persistent label to display 'Role - All'.

View stimulus in Figma

What is the error in this Blueline annotation for the CheckboxGroup and ColorSwatchPicker components?
The ColorSwatchPicker is not interactive
There should be 14 tab stops specified
The CheckboxGroup is NOT a single tab stop
The ColorSwatchPicker is NOT a single tab stop
Correct! Checkboxes are independent controls; each is its own tab stop. The single-tab-stop pattern applies to radio groups, not checkboxes.
Incorrect. The ColorSwatchPicker is interactive.
Incorrect. 14 individual tab stops would be poor keyboard design.
Incorrect. The ColorSwatchPicker correctly uses single tab stop like radio groups.

View stimulus in Figma

The designer set autofocus to the positive action. Where should the first tab stop be in this modal?
The close button
The tab group
See all photos button
It's correctly applied to the free trial button
Correct! Focus should move to the first focusable element (close button) per SC 2.4.3 Focus Order Focus Order.
Incorrect. The close button should receive focus first as the escape mechanism.
Incorrect. Focus should begin at the close button, not deep in the modal.
Incorrect. Autofocusing the CTA violates expected focus order patterns.

View stimulus in Figma

Measure the color handle size. Which SC 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum) Target Size exception applies to consider this conforming?
The handle size is determined by the user agent
The handle size must take into consideration the size of the track's hit area
The handle size is constrained by the line-height of non-target text
The handle size is "essential" to the presentation
Correct! The track provides a larger hit area, and the numeric input offers an alternative interaction method.
Incorrect. This is a custom slider, not a user agent control.
Incorrect. Slider handles are not inline text elements.
Incorrect. The handle size is not essential; it could be larger.

item.title: Q18: Sticky Elements at High Zoom

material.mattext:
View stimulus in Figma

What is the root cause of the poor user experience at 400% shown here?
The page resolution becomes too pixelated on non-vector graphics at high zoom levels
The accessible names on the navigation icons are no longer readable
Sticky elements consume the viewport, leaving insufficient space for content and causing overlaps
The page fails to reflow content into a single-column layout and requires horizontal scrolling
Correct! Sticky elements dominate the reduced viewport at 400% zoom and fixed widgets overlap controls.
Incorrect. Pixelation is not an accessibility issue addressed by WCAG.
Incorrect. Accessible names are programmatic and unaffected by zoom.
Incorrect. The content is reflowing correctly; sticky elements are the problem.

item.title: Q19: Text Spacing and Container Sizing

material.mattext:
View stimulus in Figma

When a user employs spacing preferences, button text is getting clipped. Which option conforms?
Option A as the container height is set to hug
Option B as the container width is set to hug
Both A and B can be considered conforming options
Neither A nor B is a conforming option
Correct! SC 1.4.1 Use of Color2 requires no loss of content. Both options preserve the full label and maintain operability.
Partially correct. Option A works, but Option B is equally valid.
Partially correct. Option B works, but Option A is equally valid.
Incorrect. Both options conform by preserving the complete label.

View stimulus in Figma

When a user rotates their phone, Acrobat Reader switches to landscape. Which statement is true?
This is a problem for users with motion sensitivity as the movement is disorienting
This is a benefit for users who have mobile devices mounted to their wheelchairs in a fixed orientation
This feature is meant to support 200% and 400% zoom by reflowing based on user preference
This is a problem for low vision users, as the viewport should have restricted the orientation to portrait
Correct! SC 1.3.4 Orientation Orientation requires supporting both orientations for users with mounted devices.
Incorrect. User-initiated rotation is expected behavior, not a motion sensitivity issue.
Incorrect. Orientation and reflow are separate WCAG requirements.
Incorrect. Restricting orientation would violate SC 1.3.4 Orientation.

View stimulus in Figma

What could you call out regarding the landmark annotations?
You cannot have the first nav landmark be a child of, or nested within, the header landmark
When you have more than one landmark type, you have to use different colored annotation markers
You cannot have the first nav landmark be a child of, or nested within, the main landmark
When you have more than one landmark type, you must provide an accessible name for both
Correct! Multiple landmarks of the same type need accessible names to differentiate them per SC 1.3.6 Identify Purpose.
Incorrect. Nav landmarks can be nested within header landmarks.
Incorrect. Marker colors don't affect accessibility conformance.
Incorrect. The nav landmarks are not nested within main in this interface.

View stimulus in Figma

Which WCAG success criterion addresses inconsistent identification of same-function components?
SC 3.2.4 Consistent Identification Consistent Identification (Level AA)
SC 3.2.3 Consistent Navigation Consistent Navigation (Level AA)
SC 3.2.5 Change on Request Change on Request (Level AAA)
SC 3.2.6 Consistent Help Consistent Help (Level A)
Correct! SC 3.2.4 Consistent Identification requires components with same functionality to be identified consistently.
Incorrect. SC 3.2.3 Consistent Navigation addresses navigation order, not component labeling.
Incorrect. SC 3.2.5 Change on Request is about context changes, not labeling.
Incorrect. SC 3.2.6 Consistent Help is specifically about help features.

View stimulus in Figma

If this interface had a skip to main link, the user activated it, then hit Tab, which element would gain focus?
The search bar
Healthcare keyword
The first icon
The Back button
Correct! Skip to main moves focus to main content, then Tab goes to the first interactive element: the search bar.
Incorrect. The search bar comes before the keywords in DOM order.
Incorrect. The icon grid comes after the search bar and keywords.
Incorrect. The Back button is in the footer, not main.

View stimulus in Figma

When the page loads, the animation shows a play button (paused by default). Why?
It's non-conforming to have an animation play on page load
The marketing team finds that people enjoy pressing play and pause buttons
The user has reduced motion enabled, and the page honors that preference
The page is showcasing a product's ability to create animated content
Correct! The page honors prefers-reduced-motion via CSS media query, aligning with SC 2.3.3 Animation from Interactions.
Incorrect. WCAG allows auto-play if pause/stop controls are provided.
Incorrect. This is an accessibility accommodation, not a marketing choice.
Incorrect. The paused state is due to user's reduced motion preference.

View stimulus in Figma

The product team proposes a 'Flicker' animation feature. What advice would you offer?
Ensure the flashing content flashes at least three times per second and displays the preview in red
Ensure the preview is a 341 x 256 pixel block representing a 10-degree viewport
Nothing, the standard refers to flashing and blinking, which is flickering and is therefore out of scope
At a minimum, add a warning for flashing content, do not trigger on hover, and consider not publishing this feature
Correct! Flashing content poses seizure risks under SC 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold and 2.3.2. Warnings and reconsidering the feature are responsible advice.
Incorrect. Content must NOT flash more than three times per second. Red is particularly dangerous.
Incorrect. Size doesn't make flashing safe. Flash rate and intensity are the concerns.
Incorrect. Flicker, flash, and blink are all covered by WCAG's flashing requirements.
